question_text: Oxidation of ketones involves carbon-carbon bond cleavage. Name and write the products formed on oxidation of 2, 5-dimethylhexan-3-one.
question_html: Oxidation of ketones involves carbon-carbon bond cleavage. Name and write the products formed on oxidation of 2, 5-dimethylhexan-3-one.
solution_text: Being unsymmetrical ketone oxidation involves the C-C bond cleavage, oxidation occurs on either side of the C=O group giving a mixture of 2-methyl propanoic acid, 3-methylbutanoic acid, and propan 2-one. Propan-2- one on further oxidation gives a mixture of ethanoic acid and methanoic acid. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{C{H_3}} } H - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - C{H_2} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{C{H_3}} } H - C{H_3}}\limits_{2,5 - Dimethylhexan - 3 - one} \xrightarrow{{[O]}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{C{H_3}} } H - C{H_2} - COOH}\limits_{3 - Methylbu\tan oic\,\,acid} + \mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - C{H_3}}\limits_{\Pr opan - 2 - one} {/tex} {tex} \to \mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_{Ethanoic\,\,acid} + \mathop {HCOOH}\limits_{Methanoic\,\,acid}{/tex}
solution_html:
Being unsymmetrical ketone oxidation involves the C-C bond cleavage, oxidation occurs on either side of the C=O group giving a mixture of 2-methyl propanoic acid, 3-methylbutanoic acid, and propan 2-one. Propan-2- one on further oxidation gives a mixture of ethanoic acid and methanoic acid. 
{tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{C{H_3}} } H - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - C{H_2} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{C{H_3}} } H - C{H_3}}\limits_{2,5 - Dimethylhexan - 3 - one} \xrightarrow{{[O]}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{C{H_3}} } H - C{H_2} - COOH}\limits_{3 - Methylbu\tan oic\,\,acid} + \mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - C{H_3}}\limits_{\Pr opan - 2 - one} {/tex}{tex} \to \mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_{Ethanoic\,\,acid} + \mathop {HCOOH}\limits_{Methanoic\,\,acid}{/tex}

question_text: Arrange the following in decreasing order of their acidic strength and give a reason for your answer: CH 3 CH 2 OH, CH 3 COOH, ClCH 2 COOH, FCH 2 COOH, C 6 H 5 CH 2 COOH
question_html:
Arrange the following in decreasing order of their acidic strength and give a reason for your answer:
CH3CH2OH, CH3COOH, ClCH2COOH, FCH2COOH, C6H5CH2COOH
solution_text: Decreasing order of acidic strength of the given compounds is as follows: FCH 2 COOH > ClCH 2 COOH > C 6 H 5 CH 2 COOH > CH 3 COOH > CH 3 CH 2 OH Explanation: Effect of substituents on the acidity of carboxylic acids: Substituents may affect the stability of the conjugate base and thus, also affect the acidity of the carboxylic acids. Electron withdrawing groups increase the acidity of carboxylic acids by stabilising the conjugate base through delocalisation of the negative charge by inductive and/or resonance effects. Conversely, electron-donating groups decrease the acidity by destabilising the conjugate base.
solution_html:
Decreasing order of acidic strength of the given compounds is as follows:
FCH2COOH > ClCH2COOH > C6H5CH2COOH > CH3COOH >  CH3CH2OH
Explanation: Effect of substituents on the acidity of carboxylic acids: Substituents may affect the stability of the conjugate base and thus, also affect the acidity of the carboxylic acids. Electron withdrawing groups increase the acidity of carboxylic acids by stabilising the conjugate base through delocalisation of the negative charge by inductive and/or resonance effects. Conversely, electron-donating groups decrease the acidity by destabilising the conjugate base.

question_text: Arrange the following in decreasing order of their acidic strength. Give an explanation for the arrangement: C 6 H 5 COOH, FCH 2 COOH, NO 2 CH 2 COOH
question_html:
Arrange the following in decreasing order of their acidic strength. Give an explanation for the arrangement:
C6H5COOH, FCH2COOH, NO2CH2COOH
solution_text: Decreasing order of acidic strength is as follows: NO 2 CH 2 COOH > FCH 2 COOH > C 6 H 5 COOH -NO 2 and -F groups are electron-withdrawing groups. Out of the two -NO 2 has a greater electron-withdrawing effect. C 6 H 5 group has an electron denoting effect.
solution_html:
Decreasing order of acidic strength is as follows: NO2CH2COOH > FCH2COOH > C6H5COOH
-NO2 and -F groups are electron-withdrawing groups. Out of the two -NO2 has a greater electron-withdrawing effect. C6H5 group has an electron denoting effect.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Alkenes and carbonyl compounds , both contain a {tex}\pi{/tex} bond but alkenes show electrophilic addition reactions whereas carbonyl compounds show nucleophilic addition reactions. Explain.
question_html: Alkenes  and carbonyl compounds , both contain a {tex}\pi{/tex} bond but alkenes show electrophilic addition reactions whereas carbonyl compounds show nucleophilic addition reactions. Explain.
solution_text: Carbonyl group is polar in nature. Due to larger electronegativity of oxygen, as compared to carbon, carbon acquires partial positive charge while Oxygen acquires a partial negative charge Because of a slight positive charge on C atom, it is attacked by nucleophiles and, therefore undergoes nucleophilic addition reaction. An ethene (alkenes) double bond is a non-polar bond and is a source of electrons. Therefore, it is attacked by electrophiles and undergoes electrophilic addition reactions despite the fact that both contain {tex}\pi{/tex} bond.
solution_html:
Carbonyl group is polar in nature. Due to larger electronegativity of oxygen, as compared to carbon, carbon acquires partial positive charge while Oxygen acquires a partial negative charge

Because of a slight positive charge on C atom, it is attacked by nucleophiles and, therefore undergoes nucleophilic addition reaction.
An ethene (alkenes) double bond is a non-polar bond and is a source of electrons. Therefore, it is attacked by electrophiles and undergoes electrophilic addition reactions despite the fact that both contain {tex}\pi{/tex} bond.

question_text: Carboxylic acids contain carbonyl group but do not show the nucleophilic addition reaction like aldehydes or ketones. Why?
question_html: Carboxylic acids contain carbonyl group but do not show the nucleophilic addition reaction like aldehydes or ketones. Why?
solution_text: It is due to resonance as shown below. The partial positive charge on the carbonyl carbon atom is reduced. Similarly, a carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones may be regarded as the resonance hybrid of the following structures. The carbonyl carbon of the carboxylic group is less electrophilic than carbonyl carbon in aldehydes and ketones the polarity of the carbonyl group is reduced due to resonance. Hence nucleophilic addition reactions of aldehydes and ketones do not take place with carboxylic acids.

Similarly, a carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones may be regarded as the resonance hybrid of the following structures.

question_text: Why are carboxylic acids more acidic than alcohols or phenols although all of them have hydrogen atom attached to a oxygen atom (-O-H)?
question_html: Why are carboxylic acids more acidic than alcohols or phenols although all of them have hydrogen atom attached to a oxygen atom (-O-H)?
solution_text: The aliphatic carboxylic acids are stronger acids than alcohols and phenols The difference in the relative acidic strengths can be understood if we compare the resonance hybrids of carboxylate ion and ethoxide phenoxide ion. RCOOH {tex} \rightleftharpoons{/tex} RCOO - + H + ROH {tex} \rightleftharpoons{/tex} RO - + H + The resonance hybrids may be represented as: The resonance hybrids may be represented as: Carboxylate ion Phenoxide ion (No resonance) The conjugate base of phenol, a phenoxide ion, has non-equivalent resonance structures in which the negative charge is at the less electronegative carbon atom. Therefore, the negative charge is delocalised over two electronegative oxygen atoms in carboxylate ion whereas it is less effectively delocalised over one oxygen atom and less electronegative carbon atoms in phenoxide ion. Thus, the carboxylate ion is more stabilised than phenoxide ion, so carboxylic acids are more acidic than phenols.
solution_html:
The aliphatic carboxylic acids are stronger acids than alcohols and phenols The difference in the relative acidic strengths can be understood if we compare the resonance hybrids of carboxylate ion and ethoxide phenoxide ion. 
RCOOH {tex} \rightleftharpoons{/tex} RCOO- + H+ 
ROH {tex} \rightleftharpoons{/tex} RO- + H+

Carboxylate ion Phenoxide ion (No resonance) The conjugate base of phenol, a phenoxide ion, has non-equivalent resonance structures in which the negative charge is at the less electronegative carbon atom. Therefore, the negative charge is delocalised over two electronegative oxygen atoms in carboxylate ion whereas it is less effectively delocalised over one oxygen atom and less electronegative carbon atoms in phenoxide ion. Thus, the carboxylate ion is more stabilised than phenoxide ion, so carboxylic acids are more acidic than phenols.

book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Can Gatterman-Koch reaction be considered similar to Friedel Craft’s acylation? Discuss.
question_html: Can Gatterman-Koch reaction be considered similar to Friedel Craft’s acylation? Discuss.
solution_text: Gattermann-Koch reaction is formylation (introduction of -CHO group) to the benzene nucleus i.e benzaldehyde by treating with anhydrous AlCl ​​​3 . Friedel-Crafts acylation reaction is the introduction of RCO- group in the benzene ring. In Friedel-Crafts acylation reactions, the benzene is treated with an acid chloride in the presence of anhydrous AlCl 3 . So, Gattermann-Koch reaction can be considered similar to Friedel-Crafts acylation reaction.
solution_html:
Gattermann-Koch reaction is formylation (introduction of -CHO group) to the benzene nucleus i.e benzaldehyde by treating with anhydrous AlCl​​​3.
Friedel-Crafts acylation reaction is the introduction of RCO- group in the benzene ring.

In Friedel-Crafts acylation reactions, the benzene is treated with an acid chloride in the presence of anhydrous AlCl3. So, Gattermann-Koch reaction can be considered similar to Friedel-Crafts acylation reaction.

question_text: An alkene A (Mol. formula C 5 H 10 ) on ozonolysis gives a mixture of two compounds B and C. Compound B gives positive Fehling’s test and also forms iodoform on treatment with I 2 and NaOH. Compound C does not give Fehling’s test but forms iodoform. Identify the compounds A, B and C. Write the reaction for ozonolysis and formation of iodoform from B and C.
question_html: An alkene A (Mol. formula C5H10) on ozonolysis gives a mixture of two compounds B and C. Compound B gives positive Fehling’s test and also forms iodoform on treatment with I2 and NaOH. Compound C does not give Fehling’s test but forms iodoform. Identify the compounds A, B and C. Write the reaction for ozonolysis and formation of iodoform from B and C.
solution_text: Compound B gives positive Fehling's test. It shows that it is an aldehyde and gives an iodoform test which shows it has -COCH 3 group. Compounds C is a ketone because it does not give Fehling's test but gives the iodoform test which shows it also has -COCH 3 groups. Hence compound A is {tex}C{H_3}CH = \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{C{H_3}} } - C{H_3}{/tex} , B is CH 3 -CHO and compound C is O=C(CH 3 )CH 3 {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}CH = \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{C{H_3}} } - C{H_3}}\limits_{2 - Methylbut - 2 - ene} \xrightarrow[{(ii)\,\,Zn/{H_2}O}]{{(i)\,\,{O_3}}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - CHO}\limits_{\mathop {Acetaldehyde}\limits_{\mathop {(Ethanal)}\limits_{(B)} } } + \mathop {O = \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{C{H_3}} } - C{H_3}}\limits_{\mathop {(Acetone)}\limits_{(C)} } {/tex} other isomers of (A) will not give products corresponding to the given test (2NaOH + I 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} NaOI + H 2 O) {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}CHO}\limits_{(B)} + \mathop {3NaOI}\limits_{\mathop {Sodium}\limits_{Hypoidite} } \to \mathop {C{l_3}CHO}\limits_{\mathop {Tri - iodo}\limits_{acetaldehyde} } + 3NaOH{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}CHO}\limits_{Iodal} + NaOH\xrightarrow{{Hydrolysis}}\mathop {CH{I_3}}\limits_{Iodoform} + HCOONa{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - C{H_3}}\limits_{(C)} + 3NaOI \to \mathop {C{l_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - C{H_3}}\limits_{Tri - iodoacetone} + 3NaOH{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - C{H_3} + NaOH\xrightarrow{{Hydorlysis}}\mathop {CH{I_3}}\limits_{Iodoform} + \mathop {C{H_3}COONa}\limits_{Sodium\,\,acetate} {/tex}
solution_html:
Compound B gives positive Fehling's test. It shows that it is an aldehyde and gives an iodoform test which shows it has -COCH3 group. Compounds C is a ketone because it does not give Fehling's test but gives the iodoform test which shows it also has -COCH3 groups.
Hence compound A is {tex}C{H_3}CH = \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{C{H_3}} } - C{H_3}{/tex} , B is CH3-CHO and compound C is O=C(CH3)CH3

    other isomers of (A) will not give products corresponding to the given test 
    (2NaOH + I2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} NaOI + H2O)
  2. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}CHO}\limits_{(B)} + \mathop {3NaOI}\limits_{\mathop {Sodium}\limits_{Hypoidite} } \to \mathop {C{l_3}CHO}\limits_{\mathop {Tri - iodo}\limits_{acetaldehyde} } + 3NaOH{/tex} 
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}CHO}\limits_{Iodal} + NaOH\xrightarrow{{Hydrolysis}}\mathop {CH{I_3}}\limits_{Iodoform} + HCOONa{/tex}

question_text: An aromatic compound A (Molecular formula C 8 H 8 O) gives positive 2, 4-DNP test. It gives a yellow precipitate of compound B on treatment with iodine and sodium hydroxide solution. Compound A does not give Tollen’s or Fehling’s test. On drastic oxidation with potassium permanganate it forms a carboxylic acid C (Molecular formula C 7 H 6 O 2 ), which is also formed along with the yellow compound in the above reaction. Identify A, B and C and write all the reactions involved.
question_html: An aromatic compound A (Molecular formula C8H8O) gives positive 2, 4-DNP test. It gives a yellow precipitate of compound B on treatment with iodine and sodium hydroxide solution. Compound A does not give Tollen’s or Fehling’s test. On drastic oxidation with potassium permanganate it forms a carboxylic acid C (Molecular formula C7H6O2), which is also formed along with the yellow compound in the above reaction. Identify A, B and C and write all the reactions involved.
solution_text: The molecular formula of the compound is C 8 H 8 O. As A does not give Tollens or Fehling's test. It must be a ketone. It gives a positive test with 2, 4-DNP, and iodoform test. It means it is methyl ketone. B is iodoform and C is benzoic acid.

question_text: Write down functional isomers of a carbonyl compound with molecular formula C 3 H 6 O. Which isomer will react faster with HCN and why? Explain the mechanism of the reaction also. Will the reaction lead to the completion with the conversion of the whole reactant into the product at reaction conditions? If a strong acid is added to the reaction mixture what will be the effect on the concentration of the product and why?
question_html: Write down functional isomers of a carbonyl compound with molecular formula C3H6O. Which isomer will react faster with HCN and why? Explain the mechanism of the reaction also. Will the reaction lead to the completion with the conversion of the whole reactant into the product at reaction conditions? If a strong acid is added to the reaction mixture what will be the effect on the concentration of the product and why?
solution_text: C 3 H 6 O will have the following functional isomer i.e Propanal and Propan-2-one {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}CHO}\limits_{\Pr opanal(I)}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - C{H_3}}\limits_{\Pr opan - 2 - one(II)} {/tex} Compound I will react faster with HCN than compound II due to less steric hindrance and a greater positive charge on the carbon atom of the carbonyl group. Mechanism of the reaction: HCN + OH - {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CN - + H 2 O The reaction will not lead to completion since it is a reversible reaction, hence equilibrium is established. If a strong acid is added to the reaction mixture, the addition is inhibited because the formation of CN - ions from HCN is prevented.
solution_html:
C3H6O will have the following functional isomer i.e Propanal and Propan-2-one
{tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}CHO}\limits_{\Pr opanal(I)}{/tex}  {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - C{H_3}}\limits_{\Pr opan - 2 - one(II)} {/tex} 
Compound I will react faster with HCN than compound II due to less steric hindrance and a greater positive charge on the carbon atom of the carbonyl group.
Mechanism of the reaction:
HCN + OH- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CN- + H2O

The reaction will not lead to completion since it is a reversible reaction, hence equilibrium is established. If a strong acid is added to the reaction mixture, the addition is inhibited because the formation of CN- ions from HCN is prevented.
